stories.title: Standing Up for What's Right
stories.story:
During recess, Youssef noticed a younger student, no older than seven, standing near the fence while three older boys laughed and mocked him for the shoes he was wearing. The younger boy's face had gone red, and he was staring hard at the ground, clearly trying not to cry in front of everyone.

Youssef felt his stomach tighten. Getting involved meant risking those same boys turning their attention toward him instead. It would have been so much easier to keep walking, to pretend he hadn't noticed anything at all, the way most of the other students passing by had already chosen to do.

But he thought about how he would feel if nobody had ever stepped in for him. Taking a breath, Youssef walked over and stood beside the younger boy. "Leave him alone," he said, his voice steadier than he felt inside. The older boys exchanged glances, clearly surprised, and after a tense moment, they wandered off, muttering something under their breath.

The younger boy looked up at Youssef, still a little shaken. "Thank you," he said quietly. "Want to sit with me at lunch?" Youssef asked. Later, their teacher heard what had happened and told Youssef that real courage wasn't about never feeling afraid — it was about doing the right thing anyway, even when it would have been easier not to.

stories.title: The Interview
stories.story:
For her final project, Salma had to interview someone in her community about their life story. Most of her classmates chose family members, but Salma decided to ask Mr. Rafiq, the elderly man who had run the small bookshop on her street for as long as she could remember.

She felt oddly nervous walking into the shop with her notebook and list of questions. Mr. Rafiq, however, seemed delighted by the idea and invited her to sit among the tall shelves of books while he made two cups of tea.

As he spoke, Salma learned that Mr. Rafiq had opened his shop over forty years ago, back when the street had looked completely different. He told her about difficult years when few customers came in, about a fire that had once destroyed half his stock, and about his stubborn refusal to close the shop even when everyone advised him to. "Books outlast trends," he told her simply. "People always come back to them eventually."

Walking home afterward, Salma realized her project had turned into something much bigger than a school assignment. She had expected facts and dates, but instead she had learned what it meant to care about something so deeply that you protect it through decades of hardship. When she wrote her report that night, she found she had far more to say than the assignment even required.
